stem: The complete population \( \{ [[x1]],\, [[x2]],\, [[x3]],\, [[x4]],\, [[x5]] \} \) has mean \( [[mbar]] \). What is the population variance of this data set?
solution: Population variance is the mean of the squared deviations from \( \bar{x} = [[mbar]] \): \( \sigma^2 = \frac{[[ssq]]}{5} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This divides the summed squared deviations by \( n-1 = 4 \); that is the sample variance, not the population variance.
This is the total of the squared deviations without dividing by \( n = 5 \).
This is off by one from the correct variance.
This is off by one from the correct variance.

stem: The complete population \( \{ [[x1]],\, [[x2]],\, [[x3]],\, [[x4]],\, [[x5]] \} \) has mean \( [[x3]] \). What is the population standard deviation of this data set?
solution: Take the square root of the population variance: \( \sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{(-3\cdot[[k]])^2+(-[[k]])^2+0^2+[[k]]^2+(3\cdot[[k]])^2}{5}} = \sqrt{4\cdot[[k]]^2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( n-1=4 \) inside the square root; that is the sample standard deviation, not the population one.
This is the variance itself, without taking the square root.
This takes the square root of the sum of squared VALUES (not deviations from the mean) before dividing.
This is off by one from the correct standard deviation.
This is off by one from the correct standard deviation.

stem: In a data set, the value \( [[vlo]] \) occurs \( [[k]] \) times, the value \( [[v]] \) occurs \( [[m2]] \) times, and the value \( [[vhi]] \) occurs \( [[k]] \) times (no other values occur). What is the variance of this data set?
solution: The mean is \( [[v]] \) by symmetry. Weighting each squared deviation by its frequency and dividing by the total count \( [[ntot]] \): \( \frac{[[k]]([[vlo]]-[[v]])^2 + [[k]]([[vhi]]-[[v]])^2}{[[ntot]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This divides only by the count of the two extreme values, \( 2\cdot[[k]] \), ignoring the \( [[m2]] \) occurrences of the middle value.
This is the frequency-weighted sum of squared deviations without dividing by the total count.
This divides by \( n-1 \) instead of \( n \); that would be a sample-variance divisor, not appropriate here.
This forgets to weight the squared deviations by their frequency \( [[k]] \) at all.
This is off by one from the correct variance.

stem: A sample of \( [[n]] \) values has sum \( [[S]] \) and sum of squares \( \sum x_i^2 = [[Sq]] \). Using the shortcut formula \( \mathrm{Var} = \dfrac{\sum x_i^2}{n} - \bar{x}^2 \), what is the variance of this sample?
distractor_rationales: This forgets to subtract the squared mean entirely.
This reports only the squared mean, not the full shortcut expression.
This subtracts the mean itself instead of the mean squared.
This divides the sum of squares by \( n-1 \) instead of \( n \) before subtracting.
This is off by one from the correct variance.
